1:
"And he began again to teach by the sea side: and there was gathered unto him a great multitude, so that he entered into a ship." The witnesses set the scene and find meaning in the place. The Latin Bede notes that this teaching was on the same day as the foregoing sermon (Matthew 13:1), and reads the ship as "a figure of the Church, to be built in the midst of the nations." The Antiochene Chrysostom adds that Christ entered the ship that "He might not leave any one behind Him, but have all His hearers before His face," and that, though present everywhere, "He went out to us" in the economy of the flesh, "because we were not able to go to Him."

The witnesses divide gently over how far to allegorise the soils. The Greek and Latin fathers press details: Theophylact makes the Sower Christ, "the way" those "out of Christ," the thorns "cares," and the three good yields perfect, middling, and weaker lives; Bede reads the way as "a mind which is a path for bad thoughts," the birds as devils "of a heavenly origin or dwelling in the air." The grammatical-historical commentators keep to the narrative — Robertson noting Jesus "sat in the boat and taught" the pressing crowds — while Henry hears in the ship-pulpit "a presage of his sending the gospel to the isles of the Gentiles."

On the purpose of parables (vv.11-12, which the fathers expound here) a real difference appears. The Antiochene Chrysostom stresses the Sower's love for men — "the first He commends, rejects not the second, gives place to the third" — and insists that, unlike natural soil, "stony ground may become fertile," so the parable "gives us hope of repentance." The Latin Augustine reads the blindness of "those without" as deserved, "yet done in mercy to them, that they might know their sins, and being converted, merit pardon." Pseudo-Chrysostom holds that those who "did not hold fast their obedience to the law" were justly "estranged from a new teaching." So the witnesses agree that the same word that enlightens the willing hardens the careless — not by its own nature, the Reformed will add, but by their fault — while the open ear of the disciple is the appointed way into the mystery of the kingdom.
2:
"And he taught them many things by parables, and said unto them in his doctrine..." The witnesses define the parabolic method and note its double edge. The grammatical-historical Robertson observes the imperfect tense, "describing it as going on," and surveys how Mark gives only four of the parables of this day (the Sower, the Lamp, the Seed Growing of Itself, the Mustard Seed), adding (v.34) that "without a parable spake he not unto them," so that "all theories of exegesis on the basis of the number of these parables are quite beside the mark." Ellicott corrects the rendering "in his doctrine" to "in His teaching."

The Wesleyan Wesley defines a parable broadly: "not only a simile or comparison, and sometimes a proverb, but any kind of instructive speech, wherein spiritual things are explained and illustrated by natural" (Proverbs 1:6); and he draws the warning that "resting in the literal sense killeth, but the spiritual giveth life." The Puritan Henry expounds the method's purpose and peril together: parables "would tempt them to hear, for people love to be spoken to in their own language," and "careless hearers will catch at a plain comparison borrowed from common things"; yet "unless they would take pains to search into it, it would but amuse them," so that the parable was "the dark lantern" which "had a bright side toward those who applied it to themselves, but to those who were only willing for a season to play with it, gave but a flash of light now and then, and sent them away in the dark."

So the witnesses agree the parable is at once an invitation and a sieve. Its earthly form draws the multitude and lodges the truth in memory; but it yields its meaning only to those who "take pains to search into it," while it leaves the careless, as Henry says, with "the punishment of their stupidity." The same teaching that opens the kingdom to the diligent veils it from the trifler, by a method perfectly suited both to reveal and to conceal, according to the heart that hears. So the witnesses agree the parable is both a mercy and a test: an earthly form that draws the multitude and lodges the truth in memory, yielding its meaning only to the diligent who search it out, while it leaves the careless trifler, as Henry says, with the very punishment of his stupidity.
3:
"Hearken; Behold, there went out a sower to sow." The witnesses fasten on the opening summons and the identity of the Sower. The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan commentators note the emphatic call to attention: Wesley supposes Christ "probably spoke this with a loud voice, to stop the noise and hurry of the people," and Ellicott reads "the sower" with the article, "the sower" who is Christ himself. Gill identifies him: "by whom is meant Jesus Christ, who came forth from God as a teacher, and went out into the land of Judea to preach the Gospel, which is sowing spiritual things among men" — adding that the figure "may be also applicable to any faithful minister of the word."

The Antiochene Chrysostom (on the opening verses) draws out the deliberate phrase "went out to sow": a sower may go out "to break up land, or to pull up weeds, or for some other work; but this one went out to sow," scattering the seed "simply and without distinction," addressing all alike, for "the difference of the fruits follows the quality of the ground." And he marks the change of figure from the prophets, who likened God's people to a planted vine (Isaiah 5): here it is sowing, "to shew that obedience is now shorter and more easy, and will sooner yield fruit."

So the witnesses make the verse the parable's keynote. The doubled "Hearken... Behold" demands the attention the whole parable is about; and the Sower who "went out to sow" is Christ in his preaching and, after him, every minister who scatters the same seed. The accent falls, the expositors agree, not on the seed (which is everywhere the same good word) nor on the sower (who casts it impartially on all), but on the grounds that receive it — the four hearts the parable is about to distinguish, on which the whole harvest depends. The accent of the parable, the witnesses agree, falls not on the seed, which is everywhere the same good word, nor on the Sower, who casts it impartially upon all, but on the grounds that receive it — the four hearts the parable is about to distinguish, on which the whole harvest depends.
4:
"And it came to pass, as he sowed, some fell by the way side, and the fowls of the air came and devoured it up." The witnesses read the first soil as the careless, unbroken heart, and the birds as the devil who snatches the word. The grammatical-historical Lightfoot supplies the agricultural background, citing the Talmud on seed "that is cast out of the hand of the sower" or scattered from a perforated cart driven over the ploughed earth. Clarke notes a textual point, that the best manuscripts omit "of the air," the phrase perhaps borrowed from Luke 8:5.

The expositors, drawing on the interpretation Christ gives in vv.14-15, identify "the way side" as the trodden, common heart. The Puritan Henry (on the parable as a whole) explains that "when the heart, like the highway, is unploughed, unhumbled, when it lies common, to be trodden on by every passenger," then "the devil is like the fowls; he comes swiftly, and carries away the word ere we are aware." Such hearers are "careless forgetful hearers"; the word "comes in at one ear, and goes out at the other." Gill takes them as "casual and accidental hearers," who "stop awhile and hear, and so are only by the way-side."

Henry draws the practical counsel from the birds: "when these fowls come down upon the sacrifices, we should take care, as Abram did, to drive them away" (Genesis 15:11); "that, though we cannot keep them from hovering over our heads, we may not let them nestle in our hearts." So the witnesses make the way-side the picture of inattentive hearing, where the word never penetrates because the heart was never broken to receive it. The seed lies exposed on the surface of a mind too hardened and trodden to take it in, and the enemy, swift and unseen, bears it off before it can take root — the first and lightest of the failures the parable will trace, yet a fatal one, for a word that never enters can never grow. The way-side is thus the picture of inattentive hearing, where the word never penetrates because the heart was never broken to receive it; the seed lies exposed on the surface of a mind too hardened and trodden to take it in, and the enemy, swift and unseen, bears it off before it can take root.
5:
"And some fell on stony ground, where it had not much earth; and immediately it sprang up, because it had no depth of earth." The witnesses read the second soil as the shallow, emotional hearer who receives the word quickly but without root. The grammatical-historical Lightfoot draws on the agricultural setting, noting that rocky ground might have "a turf thick enough and very fruitful," but that the soil the parable supposes "wanted that thickness" — a thin skin of earth over rock, in which seed springs fast but cannot strike deep.

The expositors, following Christ's own interpretation (vv.16-17), take the stony ground for hearers in whom the word reaches only the surface affections. The Latin Bede (on v.1) reads "stone" as "the hardness of a wanton mind," and the stony places as "those breasts which are delighted only with the sweetness of the word for a time, but in a season of temptation fall away, for there is too little of healthful desire in them to conceive the seed of life." Gill takes them as those whose "hearts of stone" remain unbroken: "the word was preached to some persons who had hearts of stone, and which remained so."

The Puritan Henry (on the parable) names them among those "much affected with the word for the present, who yet receive no abiding benefit"; "the motions of soul they have are but a mere flash, like the crackling of thorns under a pot." So the witnesses make the stony ground the picture of a quick but rootless religion — a heart that welcomes the word with gladness and even springs up sooner than the rest, precisely because there is nothing beneath to nourish a lasting growth. The very speed of its springing, the expositors note, is the mark of its shallowness; for what comes up overnight, lacking depth, will wither as soon as the sun of trial is up, having no root in itself. The very speed of the springing, the expositors note, is the mark of the shallowness, for what comes up overnight, lacking depth, will wither as soon as the sun of trial is up, having no root in itself; so the stony ground figures a quick but rootless religion that welcomes the word with gladness yet has nothing beneath to nourish a lasting growth.
6:
"But when the sun was up, it was scorched; and because it had no root, it withered away." The witnesses read the scorching sun as the heat of persecution and trial, which proves the shallow hearer's want of root. Gill states it plainly: "when persecution arose because of the word, and that became very hot and vehement, it tried and pierced through this thin speculative knowledge of the word, which could not stand before it, and bear the heat of it." Barnes connects the withering to the doom of the ungodly likened to chaff (Psalm 1:4).

The expositors, following the Lord's interpretation (v.17), take the sun for "affliction or persecution for the word's sake," and the withering for the falling away of those who had received the word only in the surface affections. The Latin Bede (on v.1) had named "the sun" as "the heat of a raging persecution," against which the stony places "have no strength for fixing the root firmly." The same trial that ripens the rooted plant destroys the rootless one.

The Puritan Henry (on the parable) presses the lesson that "impressions that are not deep will not be durable, but will wear off in suffering, trying times; like footsteps on the sand of the sea, which are gone the next high tide of persecution." The fault, the witnesses agree, is not in the sun but in the soil: trial does not make the shallow hearer fall, it only reveals that he had no root to hold him. So the verse completes the second soil's portrait — a religion that flourishes in fair weather and fails in foul, withering not for want of seed or of springing, but for want of that hidden depth of grace without which no profession can endure the heat of the day. The summer sun that draws the rooted corn to ripeness scorches the rootless to death. The fault, the witnesses agree, is not in the sun but in the soil: trial does not make the shallow hearer fall, it only reveals that he had no root to hold him; the same summer heat that draws the rooted corn to ripeness scorches the rootless to death, so that a religion which flourishes in fair weather fails in foul.
7:
"And some fell among thorns, and the thorns grew up, and choked it, and it yielded no fruit." The witnesses read the third soil as the worldly heart, in which cares and riches strangle the word. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes Mark's vivid compound verb synepnixan, "squeezed together, throttled," sharper than Matthew's word, and that the detail "yielded no fruit" is Mark's alone — "barren in results." Gill takes the thorny ground for those "eaten up with the cares of the world, and the deceitfulness of riches, and other lusts."

The expositors, following Christ's interpretation (vv.18-19), identify the thorns as worldly cares, the deceit of wealth, and the lusts of other things. The Latin Bede (on v.1) explains that "riches are called thorns, because they tear the soul with the piercing of its own thoughts, and after bringing it to sin, make it bleed by inflicting a wound"; the man deceived by the desire of riches "must soon be afflicted by the toils of continual cares," and such concupiscence "chokes the word, because it does not allow a good desire to enter the heart."

The Puritan Henry (on the parable) notes that these hearers "are not openly scandalized, so as to throw off their profession, as they on the stony ground did, yet have the efficacy of it secretly choked and stifled, so that it comes to nothing; they continue in a barren, hypocritical profession." So the witnesses make the thorny ground the most dangerous soil of the three failures, because the least visible: the word here is neither snatched away nor scorched, but slowly strangled by a rival growth. The thorns of worldly care and desire spring up alongside the good seed and, sharing the same ground, rob it of room and nourishment, so that the plant lives but never ripens — a profession that endures yet bears no fruit, choked at last to barrenness by the cares it would not pull up. So the thorny ground is the most dangerous soil of the three failures, because the least visible: the word here is neither snatched away nor scorched, but slowly strangled by a rival growth of worldly care and desire that springs from the same ground, robbing the good seed of room and nourishment until the plant lives but never ripens.
8:
"And other fell on good ground, and did yield fruit that sprang up and increased; and brought forth, some thirty, and some sixty, and some an hundred." The witnesses read the fourth soil as the prepared, fruitful heart, and they note Mark's distinctive touches. The grammatical-historical Ellicott observes that Mark gives "an ascending instead of a descending scale" (thirty, sixty, a hundred, where Matthew descends), and Robertson notes the participles "growing up and increasing" peculiar to Mark, "a vivid detail enlarging on the continued growth," for "it kept on yielding as it grew" — "fruit is what matters."

Gill takes the good ground for those "whose hearts were disposed by the Spirit and grace of God to receive" the word, who "had a savoury and comfortable experience of the truths of it, it coming with power to them," so that "a good work of grace was wrought upon their souls." The good ground, the expositors agree, is not naturally good but made good by grace — for, as Gill notes elsewhere, "there is none good naturally."

The Puritan Henry (on the parable) draws the conclusion: "no good fruit is to be expected but from good seed. If the seed be sown on good ground, if the heart be humble, and holy, and heavenly, there will be good fruit, and it will abound sometimes even to a hundred fold, such a crop as Isaac reaped" (Genesis 26:12). The varying yields — thirty, sixty, a hundred — the fathers read (Theophylact, Bede) of the degrees of fruitfulness in different believers. So the witnesses make the good ground the parable's one note of hope amid three failures: a heart prepared by the Spirit, which receives the word, keeps it, and brings it through to a harvest. The fruit, not the hearing, is what proves the soil; and the great lesson, the expositors press, is that only one heart in four bears fruit, so that hearing alone, however eager, saves no one who does not bring forth the fruit of the word in life. The fruit, not the hearing, is what proves the soil, the witnesses agree; and the great lesson they press is that only one heart in four bears fruit, so that hearing alone, however eager, saves no one who does not bring forth in life the fruit of the word, a temper of mind and a course of conduct agreeable to the gospel.
9:
"And he said unto them, He that hath ears to hear, let him hear." The witnesses receive this closing call as the seal of the whole parable, demanding the very attention the parable is about. The grammatical-historical commentators note its solemnity: Gill observes Christ spoke it "to the multitude of hearers on the sea shore, among whom were all those sorts of hearers mentioned in this parable," so that each was bidden to ask which soil he was. Clarke records an ancient addition found in some copies, "and whoso understandeth, let him understand," sharpening the demand from hearing to comprehending.

The Puritan Henry draws out the force of the formula, which brackets the parable (Christ "begins, v.3, with Hearken, and concludes, v.9, with He that hath ears to hear, let him hear"): "the words of Christ demand attention, and those who speak from him may command it, and should stir it up; even that which as yet we do not thoroughly understand we must carefully attend to, believing it to be both intelligible and weighty, that at length we may understand it; we shall find more in Christ's sayings than at first there seemed to be."

Cyril of Jerusalem, in the catechetical tradition, applies the saying to the newly baptized, whose ears were anointed "that ye might receive the ears which are quick to hear the Divine Mysteries," citing this very word of the Lord. So the witnesses make the verse the parable's pointed application: it is not enough that the seed be sown and the parable spoken; the hearer must have, and must use, "ears to hear." The phrase, by its very form, divides the crowd into those who will search the parable and become good ground, and those who will let it pass as an idle tale — turning the whole teaching back upon each conscience with the question, which of the four soils am I, and have I the ears that hear? The phrase, by its very form, divides the crowd into those who will search the parable and become good ground and those who will let it pass as an idle tale, turning the whole teaching back upon each conscience with the question, which of the four soils am I, and have I the ears that truly hear?
10:
"And when he was alone, they that were about him with the twelve asked of him the parable." The witnesses note the privacy of the inquiry and that more than the Twelve were present. The grammatical-historical Robertson marks the detail as "only in Mark — a vivid recollection of Peter," and observes that Mark has "they that were about him with the twelve," where Matthew and Luke simply say "the disciples": they "did not want the multitude to see that they did not understand the teaching of Jesus." Ellicott and Clarke both note that "the presence of others besides the Twelve is directly asserted," persons who, though not styled disciples, "seriously attended to his public and private instructions."

The Puritan Henry draws the lesson from their drawing near: "they found it good to be about Christ; the nearer him the better; good to be with the twelve, to be conversant with those that are intimate with him." The withdrawal from the crowd to question Christ in private is, for Henry, the very posture of the teachable, who are not content to hear a parable but seek its meaning from the Master himself.

So the witnesses make the verse the hinge between the parable spoken to all and the interpretation given to the few. The same word that the multitude heard and let pass, the disciples — and those about them — carried back to Christ with a question; and it was this seeking, the expositors agree, that marked them as good ground and admitted them to the mystery of the kingdom. The verse thus quietly enacts the parable it follows: of all who heard, these are the ones who, having ears to hear, drew near to understand, and so received what the careless multitude went away without. To be "about him with the twelve," asking the meaning, is itself the first fruit of the seed received in an honest heart. So the verse quietly enacts the parable it follows: of all who heard, these are the ones who, having ears to hear, drew near to understand and so received what the careless multitude went away without; to be 'about him with the twelve,' asking the meaning, is itself the first fruit of the seed received in an honest heart.
11:
"Unto you it is given to know the mystery of the kingdom of God: but unto them that are without, all things are done in parables." The witnesses dwell on the gift to the disciples and the phrase "them that are without." The grammatical-historical Robertson notes that mystērion appears in the Gospels only here and the parallels, frequent in Paul; Mark's singular "mystery" covers "a wider range than growing knowledge, the permanent possession of the mystery even before they understand it," for "discipleship means initiation into the secret of God's kingdom." Lightfoot documents that "those without," in Jewish speech, named the Gentiles — "all lands besides their own" — so that Christ here "stigmatizes the Jewish nation with the very name they used of the heathen."

Clarke weighs a textual variant (the omission of "to know" in some manuscripts), and Robertson and Ellicott note the "without" as "the uninitiated, the hostile group like the scribes and Pharisees who were charging Jesus with being in league with Beelzebub." Without the key, Robertson says, "the parables are hard to understand, for parables veil the truth of the kingdom"; they are "a condemnation on the wilfully blind and hostile, while a guide and blessing to the enlightened."

The Puritan Henry presses the gift: "those who know the mystery of the kingdom of heaven must acknowledge that it is given to them; they receive both the light and the sight from Jesus Christ, who, after his resurrection, both opened the scriptures and opened the understanding" (Luke 24:27, 45). The Wesleyan Wesley reads "without" of "all obstinate unbelievers, for they shall not enter into his kingdom." So the witnesses agree that the mystery of the kingdom is a sovereign gift, granted to the disciple who draws near and withheld from the hostile who will not see — the parable serving, by one and the same word, to reveal the secret to the initiated and to leave the wilfully blind in the dark they had chosen. So the witnesses agree that the mystery of the kingdom is a sovereign gift, granted to the disciple who draws near and withheld from the hostile who will not see, the parable serving by one and the same word to reveal the secret to the initiated and to leave the wilfully blind in the dark they had chosen.
12:
"That seeing they may see, and not perceive... lest at any time they should be converted, and their sins should be forgiven them." The witnesses confront the hard quotation from Isaiah 6:9-10 and labour to fix its sense. The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes that Mark gives Isaiah's words "not as a quotation, and perhaps in a less accurate form," and that though "at first sight" they suggest "our Lord's purpose was to produce the blindness," the real meaning is that "this was to be the result of the wilful blindness of those who rejected Him; and the acceptance of a foreseen result was, in Hebrew forms of thought, expressed as the working out of an intention." Robertson agrees there is "irony" in the sad words, "the purpose of condemnation for wilful blindness and rejection such as suits the Pharisees after their blasphemous accusation," and notes Matthew softens the hina ("that") to a causal hoti ("because").

The Reformed Calvin gives the classic resolution: "the doctrine is not, strictly speaking, or by itself, the cause of blindness, but by accident." As "persons of weak sight, coming into sunshine, find their eyes dimmer, and that defect is in no way attributed to the sun but to their eyes; so, when the word of God blinds and hardens the reprobate, as this takes place through their own depravity, it belongs truly to themselves, but is accidental as respects the word." The true end of preaching, Calvin adds, "is to reconcile men to God by renewing their minds"; the hardening of the reprobate is the word's effect only through their own corruption.

The Wesleyan Wesley reads it of judicial abandonment: "now they could not see, God having given them up to the blindness which they had chosen." The Puritan Henry notes that "those only who are converted have their sins forgiven, and it is the misery of unconverted souls that they lie under unpardoned guilt." So the witnesses, while owning the difficulty of the words, agree in their substance: the parables did not create the blindness but confirmed it; the same sun that gives sight to the seeing dims the diseased eye, and the wilful rejection of the light is justly met by its withdrawal, so that men who would not see are at last given over to the blindness they preferred.
13:
"Know ye not this parable? and how then will ye know all parables?" The witnesses treat the Lord's question as a gentle rebuke and a key to all that follows. The grammatical-historical commentators note that the question is peculiar to Mark (Clarke: "this verse is not found in any of the other evangelists"). Robertson reads it as implying "surprise at their dulness though initiated into the secret of God's Kingdom," for "incapacity to comprehend this parable of the sower raises doubt about all the others." Ellicott hears in it Christ "contemplating for His disciples an ever-growing insight," the Sower being "a pattern and exemplar of the method of interpretation" — the first lesson in reading every parable.

The Puritan Henry expounds the rebuke in two directions. First, by difficulty: "if ye know not this, which is so plain, how will ye understand other parables which will be more dark? If ye are run aground with this, how will ye understand the parables which will speak of the rejection of the Jews and the calling of the Gentiles?" — quoting "if thou hast run with the footmen, and they have wearied thee, then how shalt thou contend with horses?" (Jeremiah 12:5). Second, by function: "this parable is to teach you to be attentive to the word, that you may understand it; if ye receive not this, ye will not know how to use the key by which ye must be let into all the rest."

Henry adds that "before Christ expounds the parable, he shows them how sad their case was who were not let into the meaning, and what a shame it was that they needed such particular explaining" — for "those that would improve in knowledge must be made sensible of their ignorance." So the witnesses make the verse the threshold of the interpretation: the disciples are mildly reproved that they, who have the key, should still need the lock opened for them; and the parable of the sower is named as the master-parable, the one whose meaning, once grasped, teaches how every other is to be read. To miss this plainest of parables is to be unfit for the harder; to learn it is to hold the key to all.
14:
"The sower soweth the word." The witnesses receive this opening line of the interpretation as the clue to the whole, and they dwell on the dignity of "the word" that is sown. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes that Mark's clause is "not put thus clearly and simply in Matthew or Luke" — the seed is the word, plainly named. Gill expounds the word sown as "the word of life and truth; the word of peace and reconciliation; the word of faith and righteousness; the word of salvation; the word which publishes and declares all these to be in and by Jesus Christ."

The Puritan Henry draws out the impartial scattering: "in the great field of the church, the word of God is dispensed to all promiscuously; the sower soweth the word, sows it at a venture, beside all waters, upon all sorts of ground (Isaiah 32:20), not knowing where it will light, or what fruit it will bring forth." Christ "was awhile sowing himself, when he went about teaching; now he sends his ministers, and sows by their hand"; ministers "are sowers; they have need of the skill and discretion of the husbandman" (Isaiah 28:24), looking up to God "who gives seed to the sower" (2 Corinthians 9:10).

So the witnesses make the verse the foundation of the interpretation: every soil that follows is a heart, and the one seed cast on all of them is the word of God. The Sower is Christ and, after him, his ministers; the seed is everywhere the same good and saving word; and the whole difference of the harvest, the expositors press, lies not in the seed but in the ground. By naming the seed "the word," Christ fixes the parable's meaning beyond mistaking: it is a parable about hearing, about what becomes of the gospel in the various hearts to which it is preached, and about the solemn truth that the same word which saves one will be lost in another, according to the soil that receives it. By naming the seed 'the word,' Christ fixes the parable's meaning beyond mistaking: it is a parable about hearing, about what becomes of the gospel in the various hearts to which it is preached, and about the solemn truth that the same word which saves one will be lost in another, according to the soil that receives it.
15:
"And these are they by the way side... when they have heard, Satan cometh immediately, and taketh away the word that was sown in their hearts." The witnesses expound the first soil interpreted, and they note Mark's distinctive phrases. The grammatical-historical Robertson observes that "where the word is sown" is "explanatory detail only in Mark," that Mark names the snatcher "Satan" where Matthew has "the evil one" and Luke "the devil," and that "sown in them" means "within them, not just among them." Ellicott notes the better manuscripts read simply "in them" rather than "in their hearts." Clarke records an old reading, that these "receive the word negligently," and asks the reader, "art thou one of them?"

Gill takes these hearers as "casual and accidental hearers" of the word, "who, coming where the Gospel is preached, stop awhile and hear it, and so are only by the way-side." The seed never penetrates because the heart is trodden and unbroken; and Satan, swift as the birds, bears it off before it can lodge.

The expositors, here as on v.4, make the way-side the picture of inattentive hearing, on which the enemy works most freely. The Puritan Henry had observed that "the devil is very busy about loose, careless hearers, as the fowls of the air go about the seed that lies above ground." So the witnesses make the verse the interpretation of the lightest yet most immediate failure: the word, heard without attention, is gone "immediately," snatched by Satan before any impression is made. The fault lies in the unploughed heart, common and hardened, on which the seed cannot but lie exposed; and the swiftness of the loss — Mark's "immediately" — marks how readily the gospel is lost on those who hear it only with the surface of the mind, while the enemy stands ever ready to carry off what was never taken in. So the way-side interpreted is the picture of the lightest yet most immediate failure: the word, heard without attention, is gone 'immediately,' snatched by Satan before any impression is made, the fault lying in the unploughed heart, common and hardened, on which the seed cannot but lie exposed.
16:
"And these are they likewise which are sown on stony ground; who, when they have heard the word, immediately receive it with gladness." The witnesses expound the second soil interpreted, the shallow hearer whose gladness has no root. Gill, who carries the comment here, describes them precisely: "such sort of hearers are signified by the stony ground, who are constant hearers of the word, and have some understanding of it, and some sort of affection for it, and yet their hearts are not truly broken by it; they are not brought to a thorough sight and sense of sin, and of their need of Christ; their stony hearts are not taken away, and hearts of flesh given them."

The mark of this soil, the expositors agree across vv.5-6 and 16-17, is the very speed and gladness of the reception. Where the way-side hearer is inattentive, this one is eager — he "receives it with gladness" and "immediately," springing up sooner than the rest. But the gladness is in the surface affections only, not in a heart broken and renewed; and what springs up so fast, having nothing beneath, must wither as fast when trial comes.

So the witnesses make the verse a warning against a religion of mere emotion. The stony-ground hearer is not the careless but the enthusiastic, who welcomes the word with joy and makes a fair profession; yet because his heart was never ploughed to the depth, because (as Gill says) his "stony heart" was never "taken away," the joy is a flash without root. The very gladness that seems so promising is, in the parable's logic, the symptom of the danger: a quick, glad, shallow reception that has not reckoned with sin or felt its need of Christ, and so cannot endure. The expositors press that true reception of the word is not less than glad, but it is more — a gladness rooted in a heart renewed, without which the brightest profession will not last the heat of the day. The very gladness that seems so promising is, in the parable's logic, the symptom of the danger; the witnesses press that true reception of the word is not less than glad but more — a gladness rooted in a heart renewed, without which the brightest profession will not last the heat of the day.
17:
"And have no root in themselves, and so endure but for a time: afterward, when affliction or persecution ariseth for the word's sake, immediately they are offended." The witnesses fasten on the want of root as the fatal defect of the second soil. Gill states it plainly: "the word has no root in their hearts, only in their natural affections; nor is the root of grace in them; there is no heart work, only speculative notions, and flashy affections." Barnes cross-references the temporary endurance of those who have "no root" (Job 27:10) and the "immediately" of their offence (2 Timothy 1:15).

The expositors, here as on v.6, take the "affliction or persecution for the word's sake" as the sun that scorches what has no root. The plant that sprang up so quickly fails as quickly when trial comes, "immediately" offended — for the trial does not create the failure but reveals the want of root that was there all along. The fault, the witnesses agree, is not in the heat but in the heart that had no depth to hold the plant.

So the verse completes the portrait of the emotional hearer. The Puritan Henry (on the parable) draws the lesson that "it is the ruin of hypocrites that they have no root; they do not act from a living fixed principle; they do not mind heart-work, and without that religion is nothing; for he is the Christian that is one inwardly." The stony-ground hearer endures "but for a time" — long enough to make a profession, not long enough to prove it — and is offended the moment the word costs him anything. The witnesses thus make the verse a searching test of profession: not whether a man receives the word with gladness, which even the rootless do, but whether there is in him that hidden "root in himself," the fixed inward principle of grace, without which the first wind of affliction will lay his religion flat. The witnesses thus make the verse a searching test of profession: not whether a man receives the word with gladness, which even the rootless do, but whether there is in him that hidden root of grace, the fixed inward principle, without which the first wind of affliction will lay his religion flat.
18:
"And these are they which are sown among thorns; such as hear the word." The witnesses expound the third soil interpreted, the worldly heart in which the word is choked. Gill, carrying the comment, describes them: "such hearers of the word are designed by the thorny ground, who are of worldly dispositions; who are immoderately careful and anxious about the things of this life, and are bent upon acquiring earthly riches, and gratifying their carnal and sensual appetites."

The thorny-ground hearer, the expositors agree across vv.7 and 18-19, differs from the two before him. He is neither inattentive like the way-side, nor merely shallow like the stony; he hears the word and lets it take root, so that it springs up and grows. His danger is not that the word is snatched or scorched, but that it is slowly strangled by a rival growth springing from the same soil — the cares and desires of the world, which the parable calls thorns.

So the witnesses make the verse the introduction of the most subtle of the three failures. The thorns are not openly hostile to the word; they grow up alongside it, in a heart that gives room to both, until the worldly growth, sharing the same ground, robs the good seed of the nourishment it needs. The man hears, and believes himself fruitful, but his religion and his worldliness grow together, and the worldliness, being the stronger and the better rooted in a "worldly disposition," gradually overtops and chokes the word. The expositors press that this soil is the most dangerous precisely because it looks the most promising: the seed here is not lost at once nor in the storm, but lingers and even grows, only to be strangled at last to barrenness by the cares and pleasures the hearer would not uproot — a profession kept, but a harvest never reached. The expositors press that this soil is the most dangerous precisely because it looks the most promising: the seed here is not lost at once nor in the storm, but lingers and even grows, only to be strangled at last to barrenness by the cares and pleasures the hearer would not uproot — a profession kept, but a harvest never reached.
19:
"And the cares of this world, and the deceitfulness of riches, and the lusts of other things entering in, choke the word, and it becometh unfruitful." The witnesses expound the three thorns that strangle the word, and they note the phrase peculiar to Mark. The grammatical-historical Robertson observes that "the lusts of other things" — hai peri ta loipa epithumiai — covers "all the passions or longings, sensual, worldly," the "pleasures of this life" that Luke names (Luke 8:14), "the world of sense drowning the world of spirit"; yet the word epithumia "is not evil in itself," for one may yearn for "what is high and holy" (Luke 22:15; Philippians 1:23). Gill takes the three thorns as worldly cares that "fill the mind even while they are hearing the word," riches "which are deceitful, especially when trusted in," and "carnal desires after other objects" which gain "the ascendant."

The Wesleyan Wesley draws out the third thorn as "a deep and important truth": "the desire of anything, otherwise than as it leads to happiness in God, directly tends to barrenness of soul"; he notes the word "entering in" — "where they were not before" — and warns "let him who has received and retained the word see that no other desire then enter in." Clarke gathers the variant readings of the Itala: "the errors, the delights of the world... all, all, choke the word!"

So the witnesses make the verse the anatomy of worldliness in the soul. Three thorns are named — anxious care for this life, the deceitful trust in riches, and the craving for "other things" — and all three, the expositors agree, do their work not by open enmity but by crowding the heart and "choking" the word, so that it "becometh unfruitful." The plant is not killed but rendered barren; it lives and grows yet bears nothing, strangled by a rival growth the hearer cherished. The lesson the witnesses press is the danger of the divided heart: a religion real enough to spring up, but choked at last by the cares and desires that were never pulled up, so that the word, crowded out of room and life, comes to nothing.
20:
"And these are they which are sown on good ground; such as hear the word, and receive it, and bring forth fruit, some thirtyfold, some sixty, and some an hundred." The witnesses expound the fourth soil interpreted, the only one that bears fruit, and note its threefold marks. The grammatical-historical Robertson observes that "bear fruit" is the same verb (karpophorousin) as in the parallels, and that Mark gives the ascending order, thirty, sixty, a hundred, where Matthew reverses it. Gill takes the good ground for those "who are made good men by the grace of God, for there is none good naturally."

The expositors mark the three things said of this hearer that distinguish him from the three before: he hears the word (as the way-side did not, in truth), he receives it (as the stony ground received it but rootlessly), and he brings forth fruit (which the thorny ground never reached). The Puritan Henry (on the parable) names the fruit as "a temper of mind, and a course of life, agreeable to the gospel; Christian graces daily exercised, Christian duties duly performed — this is fruit, and it will abound to our account." And he insists "no good fruit is to be expected but from good seed" on good ground, "if the heart be humble, and holy, and heavenly."

So the witnesses make the verse the parable's resolution and its hope. After three failures, one soil bears — and bears variously, some thirty, some sixty, some a hundredfold, the fathers reading the degrees of fruitfulness in different believers. The decisive mark, the expositors press, is fruit: not hearing only (which all four did in some measure), nor receiving with gladness (which the rootless did), nor even letting the word grow (which the thorny did), but bringing it through to a harvest of holy life. Henry's closing prayer voices the witnesses' aim: "let us look to the Lord, that by his new-creating grace our hearts may become good ground, and that the good seed of the word may produce in our lives those good works which are through Jesus Christ, to the praise and glory of God the Father."
21:
"Is a candle brought to be put under a bushel, or under a bed? and not to be set on a candlestick?" The witnesses read the lamp as the calling of the disciples to let their light shine, and they offer several keys. The Antiochene Chrysostom takes the candle as "that of our intellectual nature, which shines clearly or obscurely according to the proportion of our illumination," easily extinguished if "meditations which feed the light are neglected." The Latin Bede reads the bushel as "the love of this transitory life," the bed as the body in which the soul reposes, so that "he puts his light on a candlestick who employs his body in the ministry of the word." Theophylact bids the disciples "be ye a candle... not under a bed, that is, in idleness, nor under a bushel, that is, in things pertaining to the palate," but "on the eminence of a godly life."

Pseudo-Jerome reads the candle as "the discourse concerning the three sorts of seed," and the Apostles as "the candlestick whom the word of the Lord hath enlightened" — the hidden parable coming forth to light. The grammatical-historical Gill connects it to the foregoing: as the gospel is seed, so it is "a candle, the design of which is to give light to men," not to be kept a secret for ever.

The Puritan Henry gathers the sense: "those who are good ought to consider the obligations they are under to do good." Gifts and graces "make a man as a candle, the candle of the Lord (Proverbs 20:27), lighted by the Father of lights"; but "many who are lighted as candles put themselves under a bed or a bushel — they do not manifest grace themselves, nor minister grace to others." Those so lighted "should set themselves on a candlestick, improve all opportunities of doing good, as those made for the glory of God." So the witnesses agree the lamp teaches that the truth received in private (the parables expounded to the disciples) is given to be published: light is for shining, gifts are for use, and the disciple who hides his light under the bushel of worldly care or the bed of idleness betrays the very purpose for which he was kindled.
22:
"For there is nothing hid, which shall not be manifested; neither was any thing kept secret, but that it should come abroad." The witnesses read this as the reason the lamp must be set on the stand: the gospel and its gifts were never meant for concealment. The Latin Bede reads it as a charge to bold confession: "Be not ashamed of the Gospel, but amidst the darkness of persecution raise the light of the word of God upon the candlestick of your body, keeping in mind that day when the Lord will throw light upon the hidden places of darkness." Pseudo-Jerome takes "the hidden and secret thing" as "the parable of the seed, which comes forth to light when it is spoken of by the Lord."

The Antiochene Chrysostom reads it as a promise of vindication: "if ye conduct your life with care, accusation will not be able to obscure your light." The truth and the upright life cannot finally be hidden; what is now veiled, whether in parable or in obscurity, is destined to be revealed.

The Puritan Henry draws out the purpose behind the gift: "there is no treasure of gifts and graces lodged in any but with design to be communicated; nor was the gospel made a secret to the apostles, to be concealed, but that it should come abroad, and be divulged to all the world. Though Christ expounded the parables to his disciples privately, yet it was with design to make them the more publicly useful; they were taught, that they might teach." So the witnesses agree the verse grounds the duty of the lamp in the very nature of the gospel: it is a secret only for a season, entrusted to the few that it may at last reach the many. The mystery given privately to the disciples is given to be proclaimed; the light kindled in them is to be set on a stand; and the day will come — whether in the spread of the gospel now or in the final revelation of all things — when nothing hidden shall remain concealed, but every faithful light shall shine forth and every hidden truth come abroad.
23:
"If any man have ears to hear, let him hear." The witnesses receive this repeated call — the same that closed the sower (v.9) — as the seal of the sayings about the lamp and the measure, demanding the attention they require. The Latin Bede reads it of a hearing that issues in obedience: "if any man have a sense for understanding the word of God, let him not withdraw himself, let him not turn his ear to fables, but let him lend his ear to search those things which truth hath spoken, his hands for fulfilling them, his tongue for preaching them." The true hearing, that is, is not the ear only but the whole man — understanding, doing, and proclaiming the word.

Theophylact takes it pointedly of the disciples' own attention: "that none of those things which are said to you by me should escape you." The saying, repeated, presses upon the hearer that the teaching about the lamp and the measure is no idle word but a charge to be weighed and kept.

So the witnesses make the verse a recurring summons that runs through the chapter, dividing hearers as the parable of the sower had divided them. To "have ears to hear" is, in the witnesses' reading, more than to possess the faculty; it is to bring to the word that attentive, obedient, fruitful disposition which alone profits by it. The repetition, here as at v.9, turns the teaching back upon each conscience: the lamp is to be set on the stand, the measure given will be measured again, more will be given to him that hath — but only he who has ears to hear, who attends and obeys and acts, will receive the benefit. The careless, who let the saying pass as they let the parable pass, gain nothing; the diligent, who hear and keep and do, are admitted ever deeper into the mystery. The call thus stands as both invitation and warning, the same word that opens the kingdom to the willing leaving the trifler in his self-chosen deafness. The repetition, here as at verse 9, turns the teaching back upon each conscience: the same word that opens the kingdom to the willing, who attend and obey and act, leaves the trifler in his self-chosen deafness, gaining nothing from a saying he will not weigh or keep.
24:
"Take heed what ye hear: with what measure ye mete, it shall be measured to you: and unto you that hear shall more be given." The witnesses read this as a charge to weigh and use what is heard, with a promise and a warning attached. The Greek fathers connect the measure to the hearer's diligence: Theophylact glosses "with what measure ye mete" as "whatsoever degree of application ye bring, in that degree ye will receive profit." Bede reads it of reward: "if ye diligently endeavour to do all the good which ye can, and to teach it to your neighbours, the mercy of God will come in, to give you a sense to take in higher things, and a will to do better, and an everlasting reward."

The Puritan Henry expounds "take heed what ye hear" as the duty of discernment and attention: "what we hear does us no good unless we consider it; those especially that are to teach others must be very observant of the things of God." We must "take heed what we hear, by proving all things, that we may hold fast that which is good," and "be cautious, lest we be imposed upon." On the measure he offers two readings: "as we deal with God, God will deal with us — if ye be faithful servants to him, he will be a faithful Master to you"; and, "as we improve the talents we are entrusted with, we shall increase them; if we make use of the knowledge we have, for the glory of God and the benefit of others, it shall sensibly grow, as stock in trade does by being turned."

So the witnesses make the verse a law of spiritual increase. The hearer is to take heed both that he hears and what he hears, weighing and proving the word; and the measure of his diligence becomes the measure of his profit, for "unto you that hear shall more be given." Gifts and graces, the expositors agree, "multiply by being exercised"; the diligent hearer, who attends, considers, proves, and uses what he hears, finds his knowledge and grace sensibly grow, while the careless, who bring a scant measure, receive a scant return. The kingdom rewards the attentive ear and the diligent hand, giving more to those who use what they have.
25:
"For he that hath, to him shall be given: and he that hath not, from him shall be taken even that which he hath." The witnesses read this proverb as the law that gifts used are increased and gifts neglected are lost. Pseudo-Jerome expounds both halves: "he who hath faith shall have virtue, and he who hath obedience to the word shall also have the understanding of the mystery; but he who hath not faith fails in virtue, and he who hath not obedience to the word shall not have the understanding of it; and if he does not understand, he might as well not have heard." Pseudo-Chrysostom puts it of desire: "he who has the desire to hear and to seek, to him shall be given; but he who has not the desire of hearing divine things, even what he happens to have of the written law is taken from him."

The Antiochene Chrysostom adds a sharp gloss on "he that hath not": such a man "hath not" because "he has not truth," yet "he hath" in his own conceit, "because he has a lie, for every one whose understanding believes a lie thinks that he has something." The neglectful hearer's apparent possession is no real having, and even that is forfeited.

The Puritan Henry draws the practical law: "if we do not use, we lose, what we have; from him that hath not — that doeth no good with what he hath, and so hath it in vain — shall be taken even that which he hath. Burying a talent is the betraying of a trust, and amounts to a forfeiture; and gifts and graces rust for want of wearing." So the witnesses make the verse the kingdom's law of stewardship, the same that governs the talents: the diligent hearer who uses his knowledge and grace finds them multiplied, while the careless, who lets them lie unused, loses even the little he seemed to have. The saying thus seals the warning of the measure: there is no standing still in the things of God, but a continual gaining or losing, according as the hearer exercises or neglects what he has received.
26:
"So is the kingdom of God, as if a man should cast seed into the ground." The witnesses receive this parable, peculiar to Mark, of the seed growing secretly, as a picture of the silent, mysterious, certain growth of grace. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes it is "not in Matthew or Luke," one of Mark's distinctive parables. The expositors read the seed as the word, the ground as the heart or the world, and the growth as the work of God which the sower neither sees nor causes.

The Puritan Henry gives the fullest exposition. "The good seed of the gospel sown in the world, and sown in the heart, does by degrees produce wonderful effects, but without noise." It will come up, "though it seem lost and buried under the clods"; "let but the word of Christ have the place it ought to have in a soul, and it will show itself, as the wisdom from above does in a good conversation." And "the husbandman cannot describe how it comes up; it is one of the mysteries of nature" — "thus we know not how the Spirit by the word makes a change in the heart, any more than we can account for the blowing of the wind" (compare 1 Timothy 3:16, "great is the mystery of godliness").

So the witnesses make the parable a comfort to the sower and a tribute to the silent power of grace. The man casts the seed and then "sleeps and rises night and day," doing nothing toward its springing, "and yet the earth brings forth fruit of itself" by "the concurring power of the God of nature." The preacher, Henry observes, "contributes nothing" to the growth: "the Spirit of God is carrying it on when they sleep," and "the word which they preached is doing its work when they are in their graves" (Zechariah 1:5-6). The lesson the witnesses draw is patience and faith: the kingdom grows not by the sower's effort or even his understanding, but by a hidden divine working, sure and gradual, which no human eye can trace and no human hand can hasten, yet which infallibly brings the seed of the word to its harvest.
27:
"And should sleep, and rise night and day, and the seed should spring and grow up, he knoweth not how." The witnesses fasten on the sower's ignorance and inactivity as the heart of this parable. The grammatical-historical commentators note that the man "sleeps and rises" through the ordinary round of his days, "and perhaps never so much as thinks of the corn he hath sown" (Henry), yet the seed grows on without him. The growth is "he knoweth not how" — beyond the husbandman's understanding and outside his power.

The Puritan Henry presses both points. The husbandman, "when he hath sown the seed, doth nothing toward the springing of it up; he sleeps and rises, goes to sleep at night, gets up in the morning, and yet the earth brings forth fruit of itself, according to the ordinary course of nature, and by the concurring power of the God of nature." So "the word of grace, when received in faith, is in the heart a work of grace, and the preachers contribute nothing to it; the Spirit of God is carrying it on when they sleep." And he stresses the mystery: the husbandman "sees it has grown, but he cannot tell in what manner it grew, or what was the cause and method of its growth" — "thus we know not how the Spirit by the word makes a change in the heart."

So the witnesses make the verse a double lesson in humility and assurance. The sower's sleeping shows that the growth of grace does not depend on human effort or vigilance; the sower's ignorance shows that it does not depend on human understanding. The seed springs and grows by a power not the sower's own, working silently while he goes about his business and even while he sleeps. The comfort the expositors draw is that the success of the word lies not with the preacher, who can neither cause nor explain the growth, but with God, whose Spirit works the increase in his own way and time — so that the minister may sow and wait in faith, knowing that the hidden, irresistible power of grace is at work where his eye cannot follow and his hand cannot reach.
28:
"For the earth bringeth forth fruit of herself; first the blade, then the ear, after that the full corn in the ear." The witnesses read this as the law of gradual, ordered growth in the kingdom and in the soul. The grammatical-historical commentators note that "of herself" (automatē) does not mean without God, but without the sower's labour — by "the concurring power of the God of nature" (Henry). The three stages — blade, ear, full corn — mark the settled order by which grace, like nature, advances.

The Puritan Henry expounds the gradualness: "it grows gradually; when it is sprung up, it will go forward; nature will have its course, and so will grace. Christ's interest, both in the world and in the heart, is, and will be, a growing interest; and though the beginning be small, the latter end will greatly increase." He marks the stages: "at first it is but a tender blade, which the frost may nip or the foot may crush, yet it will increase to the ear, to the full corn in the ear." And he draws the maxim, "Natura nil facit per saltum — Nature does nothing abruptly; God carries on his work insensibly and without noise, but insuperably and without fail."

So the witnesses make the verse a counsel of patience with the slow work of grace. The kingdom does not leap to maturity but advances by appointed degrees, the blade before the ear and the ear before the full corn; and the same is true in the believing soul, where grace begins as a tender blade, easily threatened, yet sure to ripen if it has true root. The comfort the expositors press is twofold: that small beginnings are not to be despised, since the full corn is contained in the blade; and that the growth, though gradual and quiet, is certain, carried on by God "insuperably and without fail." The husbandman who waits through the stages, neither hastening nor doubting, will see the harvest in its season; and so the minister and the believer are to bear with the slow, ordered, irresistible ripening of the word, which God brings from blade to full ear in his own unhurried and unfailing way.
29:
"But when the fruit is brought forth, immediately he putteth in the sickle, because the harvest is come." The witnesses read the harvest as the consummation, when the ripened fruit is gathered by the Lord. The grammatical-historical commentators note the echo of Joel 3:13 ("put ye in the sickle, for the harvest is ripe"), applied in Revelation 14:15 to the final reaping. The "immediately" marks that when the fruit is ready, the gathering follows at once.

The Puritan Henry gives a double application. The harvest "intimates that Christ now accepts the services which are done to him by an honest heart from a good principle; from the fruit of the gospel taking place and working in the soul, Christ gathers in a harvest of honour to himself" (John 4:35). And it intimates "that he will reward them in eternal life; when those that receive the gospel aright have finished their course, the harvest comes, when they shall be gathered as wheat into God's barn (Matthew 13:30), as a shock of corn in his season."

So the witnesses make the verse the parable's resolution and its hope. The long, silent, gradual growth of the seed has its appointed end in a harvest; and the sower who waited through the stages, neither causing nor hastening the growth, at last "putteth in the sickle." The lesson the expositors draw is that the patient work of grace is not in vain: the word sown and grown will surely be reaped, both now, as Christ gathers the fruit of holy lives to his honour, and at the end, when the ripened saints are gathered into his garner. The parable thus answers the discouragement of the sower who sees only slow and hidden growth: let him wait in faith, for the harvest is as sure as the springing, and the same God who brings the blade to the full corn will, in his own season, bring the full corn to the sickle, gathering home the fruit of the gospel to the joy of the husbandman and the glory of the Lord of the harvest. The parable thus answers the discouragement of the sower who sees only slow and hidden growth: let him wait in faith, for the harvest is as sure as the springing, and the same God who brings the blade to the full corn will, in his season, bring the full corn to the sickle, gathering home the fruit of the gospel to the glory of the Lord of the harvest.
30:
"Whereunto shall we liken the kingdom of God? or with what comparison shall we compare it?" The witnesses receive this introduction to the mustard seed as setting forth the surpassing excellence and growth of the gospel. The Gloss notes that Christ adds this parable "to shew the excellence of the doctrine of the Gospel before all other doctrines." The deliberative question — how shall we liken it? — itself suggests that the kingdom exceeds all comparisons, yet is best figured by the smallest of seeds growing to the greatest of herbs.

The Greek fathers draw out the contrast of small beginning and great increase. Theophylact: "most brief indeed is the word of faith — Believe in God, and thou shalt be saved — but the preaching of it has been spread far and wide over the earth, and increased so that the birds of heaven, that is, contemplative men, dwell under it"; "how many wise men among the Gentiles, quitting their wisdom, have found rest in the preaching of the Gospel!" Chrysostom adds that "the wisdom spoken among the perfect expands to an extent greater than all other sayings," for "there is nothing greater than this truth." Pseudo-Jerome reads the seed as charity, "small in fear, but great when it has grown into charity, which is greater than all herbs, for God is love" (1 John 4:16).

So the witnesses make the verse the threshold of a parable about the disproportion between the gospel's humble origin and its vast extent. The kingdom of God, hard to liken because it surpasses all earthly things, is yet aptly compared to a grain of mustard seed: least in its beginning — a brief word, a small company, a despised Teacher — yet destined to spread over the whole earth and shelter the nations. The deliberative question, the expositors note, befits a mystery so great; and the comparison chosen, the tiniest of seeds, is itself the parable's point, that from the smallest and most unpromising of starts the Lord brings forth the greatest of growths, so that the very humbleness of the gospel's beginning becomes the measure of the wonder of its increase.
31:
"It is like a grain of mustard seed, which, when it is sown in the earth, is less than all the seeds that be in the earth." The witnesses read the mustard seed's smallness as the figure of the gospel's, the Church's, and grace's humble beginning. Gill takes the kingdom here for "either the Gospel, or the Gospel church state, or the grace of God in the hearts of his people, and it may include them all": the Gospel "brings life and immortality to light"; the church state, where "Christ dwells and rules as king"; and grace, "a governing principle" by which "Christ reigns in and over" his people.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the comparative "less than all the seeds" is "hyperbole, of course, but clearly meaning that from a very small seed a large plant grows, the gradual pervasive expansive power of the kingdom of God"; the smallness of the mustard seed "was proverbial," and Jesus uses it elsewhere of faith (Matthew 17:20; Luke 17:6).

Cyril of Jerusalem applies it to faith with eloquence: "just as the grain of mustard seed is small in size but fiery in its operation, and though sown in a small space has a circle of great branches and shelters the fowls; so faith in the swiftest moment works the greatest effects in the soul. For, when enlightened by faith, the soul hath visions of God, and ranges round the bounds of the universe." So the witnesses make the verse a meditation on the power hidden in the small. The mustard seed, least of seeds, is the apt figure of all that is humble and unpromising in the gospel's beginning — its brief word, its little flock, its small faith — and the parable's whole force lies in the contrast about to follow, between this least of seeds and the great herb it becomes. The lesson the expositors press is not to despise the day of small things: what looks least in the kingdom carries within it, like the mustard seed, a fiery and expansive power, so that the smallest grain of true faith or true gospel is destined to a growth out of all proportion to its beginning.
32:
"But when it is sown, it groweth up, and becometh greater than all herbs, and shooteth out great branches; so that the fowls of the air may lodge under the shadow of it." The witnesses read the mustard plant's greatness as the figure of the gospel's vast spread and sheltering power. Gill traces the growth historically: "so the Gospel, when it was preached, it spread notwithstanding all the opposition made against it by the Jews and Gentiles; there was no stopping it" — though the Sanhedrin charged the apostles to be silent, and Herod killed one apostle and imprisoned another, "the word of God grew and multiplied" (Acts 12:24); and "wherever the grace of God is really implanted, there is a growing in it."

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes that Mark's picture differs from Matthew's: where Matthew has the birds in "the branches," Mark has them "under the shadow," both using the verb "to tent or camp down," whether nesting in the branches or hopping under the shade. Barnes cross-references the prophecies of the kingdom's growth (Isaiah 11:9; Daniel 2:44; Malachi 1:11).

The Greek fathers (on v.30) read the great branches as the apostles "divided off as the boughs of a tree, some to Rome, some to India, some to other parts" (Theophylact), and the sheltering birds as "contemplative men, sublime in understanding," who find rest under the gospel. So the witnesses make the verse the climax of the parable's contrast: the least of seeds becomes the greatest of herbs, putting forth branches wide enough to shelter the birds — a figure of the gospel that, from its small and despised beginning, has spread over the whole earth and given rest to the nations under its shade. The lesson the expositors press is the certainty and extent of the kingdom's growth: opposed by Jew and Gentile, beginning in a little upper room, it has yet become a great tree in whose branches the peoples of the world find shelter, so that the very disproportion between the seed and the tree is the measure of the divine power that carries the gospel from its smallest beginnings to its world-wide harvest.
33:
"And with many such parables spake he the word unto them, as they were able to hear it." The witnesses read this summary as marking both the abundance of Christ's parables and his gracious accommodation to his hearers. The grammatical-historical commentators note that Mark records only a few of the day's parables; Gill lists the others Matthew gives (the tares, the leaven, the treasure, the pearl, the net), and Robertson observes Mark's imperfect tense, "Jesus used parables now largely, but there was a limit even to the use of them to these men."

The key phrase is "as they were able to hear it." Robertson glosses it (with John 16:12, "ye cannot bear them now") as Christ giving "the mystery of the kingdom in this veiled parabolic form which was the only feasible form at this stage." Barnes reads it of patient leading: "they were like children, and he was obliged to lead them along cautiously, and by degrees, to a full understanding of the plan of salvation." The Wesleyan Wesley draws the homiletical rule: "adapting it to the capacity of his hearers, and speaking as plain as he could without offending them — a rule never to be forgotten by those who instruct others." Clarke agrees: "always suiting his teaching to the capacities of his hearers," and adds that the preacher most useful is he "who could adapt his phrase to that of the people."

So the witnesses make the verse a lesson in the divine condescension of the Teacher. Christ did not pour out the whole mystery at once, but measured his teaching to what his hearers "were able to hear," giving the truth in parables suited to their present capacity and leading them on by degrees. The lesson the expositors draw, both of Christ and for his ministers, is the wisdom of accommodation: to teach not all that might be said, but all that can be borne; to clothe high things in homely figures; and to lead the weak forward gently, as a nurse feeds children with milk, until they are able to receive the stronger meat. The parabolic method, then, is not only a sieve dividing hearers but a mercy fitting the word to the frame of those who hear.
34:
"But without a parable spake he not unto them: and when they were alone, he expounded all things to his disciples." The witnesses read this closing summary as setting the public parable over against the private interpretation. The grammatical-historical Robertson draws out the verb: to his own disciples "in private" Jesus "was in the habit (imperfect tense) of disclosing, revealing, all things in plain language without the parabolic form used before the crowds." The verb epilyō, he notes, means "to give additional loosening, to explain, to make plainer, even to the point of revelation"; Ellicott observes that its cognate noun is the "interpretation" of 2 Peter 1:20, one of the verbal links connecting that Epistle with this Gospel.

The Wesleyan Clarke draws the lesson of fellowship: "outside hearers, those who do not come into close fellowship with the true disciples of Christ, have seldom more than a superficial knowledge of Divine things; in the fellowship of the saints, where Jesus the teacher is always to be found, everything is made plain, for the secret of the Lord is with them that fear him." Barnes notes Christ "employed this method to insinuate truth gradually into their minds, and to prepare them fully to understand the nature of his kingdom," which their prejudices made them slow to receive.

So the witnesses make the verse the seal of the chapter's two-fold teaching. To the multitude Christ spoke only in parables, veiling the mystery from the careless and the hostile; but to his own, "when they were alone," he "expounded all things," unfolding in plain words what the parable concealed. The lesson the expositors press is the privilege of discipleship and the necessity of drawing near: the same word that is a riddle to the crowd is an open secret to those who follow Christ into private and ask his meaning. The parable divides; the exposition, given only to the disciples, unites them ever more closely to the Master and admits them, by degrees, into the whole counsel of the kingdom — so that the difference between the crowd and the disciple is, at bottom, the difference between hearing a parable and being taught its meaning by Christ himself.
35:
"And the same day, when the even was come, he saith unto them, Let us pass over unto the other side." The witnesses read the crossing both historically and mystically. The grammatical-historical Robertson sets the scene: "it had been a busy day" — the blasphemous accusation, the visit of the mother and brothers, the parables by the sea and in the house — and "a row over to the eastern shore in the evening would be a delightful change and refreshing to the weary Christ; it was the only way to escape the crowds." The Antiochene Remigius notes the Lord "had three places of refuge, the ship, the mountain, and the desert," fleeing to one whenever "pressed upon by the multitude."

The fathers read the voyage typologically. Pseudo-Jerome makes "the hinder part of the ship the beginning of the Church, in which the Lord sleeps in the body only, for He never sleepeth who keepeth Israel"; "the ship is bound together by wood, that is, by the cross, and so the Church is saved; the wind and the sea are devils and persecutors, to whom He says Peace; the great calm is the peace of the Church after oppression." Bede reads the other ships as "those imbued with faith in the cross," and the whole storm as a figure of the Passion and the disciples' amazement, stilled by the Resurrection.

The Puritan Henry draws the practical notes. Christ proposed the voyage "because he would lose no time, for he has work to do" on the other side; "Christ went about doing good, and no difficulties should hinder him." The ship that had served as his pulpit "is taken under his special protection, and, though in danger, cannot sink — what is used for Christ, he will take particular care of." And he observes that they put to sea only after sending away the multitude, "for he sent none home complaining that they had attended him in vain." So the witnesses make the verse the threshold of the storm: the weary Christ, his day's teaching done, leads his disciples across the sea toward fresh work and, in the providence of God, toward a trial that would reveal his power over the deep. The crossing that begins as rest will become a tempest, and the tempest a miracle, teaching the disciples that the One who slept in their ship was the Lord of wind and wave.
36:
"And when they had sent away the multitude, they took him even as he was in the ship. And there were also with him other little ships." The witnesses fasten on the phrase "even as he was" and on the detail of the other boats, both peculiar to Mark. The grammatical-historical Ellicott reads "even as he was" as pointing "to the impression made on St. Mark's informant by the utter exhaustion that followed on the long day's labours," comparing the wearied Christ who "sat thus on the well" (John 4:6). Robertson and Clarke agree the disciples "take Jesus along without previous preparation," sailing off "just as he was in the boat out of which he had been teaching," providing neither food nor raiment for the voyage.

On the "other little ships," Robertson notes this detail "is given only by Mark" — "some people had got into boats to get close to Jesus; there was a crowd even on the lake." Barnes adds that these belonged "probably to the people, who, seeing him sail, resolved to follow him," and that they "no doubt shared in the distress and danger."

The Puritan Henry draws lessons from both details. Of "as he was": "they took him without any cloak to throw over him, which he ought to have had, to keep him warm when he went to sea at night, especially after preaching; we must not hence infer that we may be careless of our health, but we may learn not to be over nice and solicitous about the body." Of the other ships, which "carried those who were desirous to go along with Christ": "the multitude went away when he put to sea, but some there were that would venture upon the water with him; those follow the Lamb aright that follow him wherever he goes." So the witnesses make the verse a small but vivid window into the scene — the exhausted Teacher taken aboard just as he was, and a little flotilla of the devoted following him into the dark and the danger. The lesson the expositors press is the cost and the courage of true discipleship: to follow Christ across the water, sharing his risks, is the mark of those who love him, while the crowd that heard him gladly turned back at the water's edge.
37:
"And there arose a great storm of wind, and the waves beat into the ship, so that it was now full." The witnesses describe the sudden violence of the tempest and note Mark's vivid detail. The grammatical-historical Robertson gives the geography: the word lailaps denotes "these cyclonic gusts"; Luke's "came down" shows "the storm fell suddenly from Mount Hermon down into the Jordan Valley and smote the Sea of Galilee violently at its depth of 682 feet below the Mediterranean," where "the hot air draws the storm down with sudden power" — "these sudden storms continue to this day." The waves "were beating into the boat" (imperfect tense), "so that the boat was now filling," a "graphic description of the plight of the disciples." Ellicott notes both verbs "describe continuous action."

The Puritan Henry observes the severity: "the storm was so great that the ship was full of water, not by springing a leak, but perhaps partly with the shower, for the word here used signifies a tempest of wind with rain; however, the ship being little, the waves beat into it so that it was full." And he draws the lesson: "it is no new thing for that ship to be greatly hurried and endangered, in which Christ and his disciples, Christ and his name and gospel, are embarked."

So the witnesses make the verse a picture of real and deadly peril. The storm was no light squall but a sudden, violent tempest that filled the little boat and threatened to sink it; and the disciples, seasoned fishermen though some were, were brought to the edge of despair. The expositors find in it both history and figure: history, in the well-known fury of the Galilean storms; and figure, in the perils that beset the ship of the Church and the soul. The lesson Henry presses is that danger is no stranger to the company of Christ — the very ship that carried the Lord was tossed and filling — yet the presence of Christ in the ship, however it may seem to sleep, is the pledge that it cannot finally founder. The storm is permitted, the expositors agree, not to destroy but to try the disciples' faith and to prepare them for the revelation of his power.
38:
"And he was in the hinder part of the ship, asleep on a pillow: and they awake him, and say unto him, Master, carest thou not that we perish?" The witnesses dwell on the sleeping Christ and the disciples' reproachful cry, details Mark gives with peculiar vividness. The grammatical-historical commentators note the precise touches: "the stern of the boat" and "the cushion" or bolster "commonly to be found in the boat's stern" (Robertson, Clarke, Ellicott); Robertson observes Jesus "was worn out from the toil of this day."
