Ellicott marks that "carest thou not that we perish?" is "St. Mark alone," "a touch of despairing expostulation in which we trace the specific want of faith afterwards reproved."

The fathers read the sleep both literally and mystically. The Antiochene Chrysostom explains the providence of it: Christ "gives time for their fear by sleeping," for "if He had been awake, they would not have feared, nor asked Him to save them, nor thought that He could do such things"; he sleeps "shewing His humility, and teaching us many lessons of wisdom." Theophylact adds that "He allowed them to fall into the fear of danger, that they might experience His power in themselves." Pseudo-Jerome had read the pillow as "the body of the Lord, on which His Divinity has come down."

The Puritan Henry interprets the sleep as a trial of faith and a spur to prayer: "he slept, to try the faith of his disciples and to stir up prayer; upon the trial, their faith appeared weak, and their prayers strong." When the Church is in a storm, "Christ seems as if he were asleep, unconcerned in the troubles of his people; but as, when he tarries, he does not tarry (Habakkuk 2:3), so when he sleeps he does not sleep; the keeper of Israel does not slumber (Psalm 121:4)." Of their cry "carest thou not?" Henry confesses it "sounds somewhat harsh, rather like chiding him for sleeping than begging him to awake," excusable only by "the present distress that put them into such a fright that they knew not what they said." So the witnesses make the verse the heart of the trial: the Lord of the sea asleep in the storm, his frightened disciples mistaking his rest for neglect, and their desperate appeal — itself half-unbelief — about to be answered by a word that would still both the sea and their fear.
39:
"And he arose, and rebuked the wind, and said unto the sea, Peace, be still. And the wind ceased, and there was a great calm." The witnesses fasten on the majesty of the word that stilled the storm and on the deity it proved. The grammatical-historical Barnes finds the proof of Godhead in the act: "standing amidst the howling tempest, on the heaving sea, and in the darkness of the night, by his own power he stills the waves, and bids the storm subside; none but the God of the storms and the billows could awe, by a word, the troubled elements — he must therefore be Divine." Clarke marvels, "there is uncommon majesty and authority in these words; who but God could act thus?" Ellicott notes that Mark gives "the very words addressed to the raging sea, as though it were a hostile power rising in rebellion against its true Lord," and that "be still" is literally "be silenced" (the same word as the speechless guest of Matthew 22:12); "the wind lulled."

The fathers read the rebuke as the King's command. The Gloss compares it to a sovereign who "by His threats restrained the violence of the winds, and compelled the sea to be silent," the trouble of the sea being "a sort of voice threatening danger." Theophylact notes that, since Christ stilled the sea "not with a rod like Moses, nor with prayers like Elisha, nor with the ark like Joshua," but by his own bare word, the disciples "thought Him truly God."

The Puritan Henry expounds the command "Peace, be still" — "be silent, be dumb" — as both a word of command and a word of comfort. A command to us, "when our wicked hearts are like the troubled sea which cannot rest (Isaiah 57:20), when our passions are unruly: be silent, be still." And a comfort, "that, be the storm of trouble ever so strong, Jesus Christ can lay it with a word's speaking; if he say, Peace, be still, there is a great calm presently." He adds that "it is spoken of as God's prerogative to command the seas (Jeremiah 31:35); by this therefore Christ proves himself to be God — he that made the seas can make them quiet." So the witnesses make the verse the climax of the miracle and the revelation of the Person: the same Lord who slept in weariness rises in power, and by a single sovereign word brings the raging deep to instant calm, proving himself the Creator whom wind and sea obey.
40:
"And he said unto them, Why are ye so fearful? how is it that ye have no faith?" The witnesses note that the rebuke to the disciples is sharper in Mark than in Matthew. The grammatical-historical Robertson observes the two questions: "Why are ye fearful? They had the Lord of the wind and the waves with them in the boat; he was still Master even if asleep in the storm." And "Have ye not yet faith? Not yet had they come to feel that Jesus was really Lord of nature; they had accepted his Messiahship, but all the conclusions from it they had not yet drawn — how like us in our troubles they were!" Clarke presses the aggravation: they feared "having me with you," and lacked faith "having already had such proofs of my unlimited power and goodness."

The Puritan Henry marks how Mark heightens the reproof. "There it is, Why are ye fearful? here, Why are ye so fearful? though there may be cause for some fear, yet not for fear to such a degree as this. There it is, O ye of little faith; here, How is it that ye have no faith? Not that the disciples were without faith — they believed that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God; but at this time their fears prevailed so that they seemed to have no faith at all; it was out of the way when they had occasion for it, and so it was as if they had not had it." Those, he adds, "may suspect their faith who can entertain such a thought as that Christ careth not though his people perish, and Christ justly takes it ill."

So the witnesses make the verse a searching word about the failure of faith in the storm. The disciples' fault was not unbelief in Christ's person — they owned him the Son of God — but the eclipse of faith by fear at the very moment it was needed, so that a faith they truly had "was as if they had not had it." The lesson the expositors press is that genuine faith may be overborne for a season by terror, and that to suspect Christ's care in our distress is the mark of a faith asleep. The reproof is therefore not a denial that they believed, but a summons to a faith that holds fast precisely in the tempest, trusting the Master who is in the ship even when he seems to sleep.
41:
"And they feared exceedingly, and said one to another, What manner of man is this, that even the wind and the sea obey him?" The witnesses read the disciples' awe as a fear transformed and a faith growing. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the cognate accusative — "they feared a great fear" — and that, while Matthew and Luke say "they marvelled," "there was fear in it also." The question "Who then is this?" he reads as the mark of their growth: "no wonder that they feared if this One could command the wind and the waves at will; they were growing in their apprehension of Jesus Christ, they had much yet to learn." Barnes and Clarke note they were "astonished at such power proceeding from a person who appeared to be only like one of themselves."

The Puritan Henry draws out the change in their fear most fully. "In Matthew it is said, the men marvelled; here, they feared greatly — they feared a great fear. Now their fear was rectified by their faith. When they feared the winds and the seas, it was for want of the reverence they ought to have had for Christ; but now that they saw a demonstration of his power, they feared them less, and him more." This new fear "had pleasure and satisfaction in it," like that of Jonah's mariners, who, when the sea ceased its raging, "feared the Lord exceedingly, and offered a sacrifice" (Jonah 1:16). And their question "What manner of man is this?" Henry reads as a confession: "surely more than a man, for even the winds and the seas obey him."

So the witnesses make the verse the fruit of the miracle in the disciples' hearts. The terror of the storm gives way to a holier fear — the awe of those who have seen the Creator's power in their Master; and out of that awe rises the question that is half-confession, "What manner of man is this?" The lesson the expositors press is that the trial, fearful as it was, ended in a deeper knowledge of Christ: the disciples who had owned him Messiah now begin to grasp that the wind and the sea obey him as their Lord, and their rectified fear, mingled with wonder and love, gives glory to the One they had thought to be only a weary man asleep. The storm that tested their faith became the occasion of its growth, opening their eyes, as Robertson says, "to the majesty of Jesus."
